content.assessment: From an implementation-planning lens, the status-quo option requires the least delivery capacity since no new mechanism is enacted, but its 'monitoring' component depends on infrastructure that has a documented history of non-translation into action. Hungary already has assessment infrastructure (ABC testing, background analysis) yet PISA-based evidence has not historically driven structural policy change, and centralization since 2013 has not resolved core funding/decision tensions. This suggests monitoring alone, absent a feedback-to-action loop, is unlikely to alter trajectory.
opportunities: Existing infrastructure could in principle be repurposed with clearer thresholds or triggers for future action if political will shifts.
strengths: Requires no new actor coordination or capacity-building, making it trivially deliverable in the short term.
Leverages existing ABC testing infrastructure (grades 6, 8, 10) for monitoring without new system-building.
threats: Persistent structural tensions from centralization (KLIK) and unresolved funding challenges may continue regardless of monitoring, since monitoring does not address underlying capacity or governance gaps.
weaknesses: Historical precedent shows assessment data (PISA) has not translated into structural policy change despite being used for legitimization, undermining confidence that monitoring will feed into adaptation.
No described feedback loop links monitoring outputs to decision triggers, so the mechanism lacks a defined adaptation pathway.
